source_quote:
Lebeau argues the district court erred by denying his application for post-
conviction relief seeking to withdraw his guilty plea.
source: PDF pdftotext extraction; line break in source at 'post-' caused improper spacing when joined
evidence: The DB text contains 'post- conviction' (with space before 'conviction') exactly once. The source PDF has 'post-' at end of line 34 and 'conviction relief' at start of line 35. The pdftotext join preserved the space, creating 'post- conviction' instead of the correct 'post-conviction'. All instances of 'post-conviction' (without space) appear correctly elsewhere in the text, confirming this is a join artifact.
note: This is a mechanical OCR/PDF extraction error where a hyphenated word split across lines was not properly rejoined. The correct form appears elsewhere in the same opinion.

source_quote:
collided with a
horse-drawn hay trailer. The collision killed one of the five passengers on the hors
evidence: The DB text contains 'horse- drawn' (incorrectly split with a space before 'drawn'), while the authoritative PDF source shows 'horse-drawn' as a properly hyphenated compound adjective. This is an ingestion/OCR defect where the hyphenated word was incorrectly separated. The fix joins the word back to its proper form, which appears exactly once in the DB and is clearly present in the source.

source_quote: Osborne v. Brown & Saenger, Inc., 2017 ND 288, ¶ 13, 904 N.W.2d 34.
evidence: The DB text contains 'Brown &Saenger' (missing space after ampersand). The authoritative PDF source clearly shows 'Brown & Saenger' (with space before and after the ampersand) in the footnote citation to Osborne v. Brown & Saenger, Inc., 2017 ND 288. Visual inspection of rendered PDF page 9 confirms the proper spacing. This is a standard citation formatting requirement.

source_quote:
David Gray appeals from a judgment entered after a jury found him
guilty of preventing arrest. He ar
evidence: PDF rendered glyph shows 'David' as single word on rendered page. DB shows split 'Dav id' which is an OCR/ingestion artifact where word boundary was incorrectly introduced, splitting the name across a token boundary. The opening paragraph [¶1] clearly shows 'David Gray appeals' in both the rendered PDF and pdftotext output; only the DB has the erroneous split.
note: This is a clear OCR/text-extraction defect where a single name was split into two tokens. The PDF authentically shows 'David' as one word.

evidence: The authoritative PDF shows the justices' signatures on separate lines with indentation: Lisa Fair McEvers on the first line, then Daniel J. Crothers, Jerod E. Tufte, Jon J. Jensen, and Gerald W. VandeWalle, C.J. each on their own line with 6-space indentation. The database collapsed all five justices into a single line separated by spaces, which is an ingestion error.
note: This is a multiline signature block formatting error. The PDF's layout formatting (separate lines with indentation) was lost during database ingestion, with all justices collapsed onto a single line.

source_quote: [¶2]   Gerald W. VandeWalle, C.J.
evidence: The DB text contains 'Vande Walle' (with space), but the authoritative PDF source consistently shows 'VandeWalle' (no space). This was verified by (1) pdftotext extraction showing 'VandeWalle', (2) rendered image (mutool draw) of page 2 clearly showing 'VandeWalle, C.J.' with no space, and (3) byte-exact verification in both files. Chief Justice Gerald W. VandeWalle's surname is properly spelled as one word in the published opinion.
note: This is the only substantive difference between the DB and source versions (exclusive of intentional quote-character normalization and header metadata reformatting). The opinion body text is otherwise identical.

source_quote: [¶3]   Gerald W. VandeWalle, C.J.
evidence: The authoritative PDF source shows the Chief Justice's name as a single word 'VandeWalle', not as two words 'Vande Walle'. The name appears once in the DB and once in the source, both occurrences matching the pattern exactly except for the spacing.
